title: Empire
summary: The political form in which one state rules over multiple peoples who did not choose that rule — the dominant political structure of most of recorded history, the framework through which most of the world's wealth, knowledge, and people were connected, and the system whose legacies continue to shape international politics, economic inequality, and cultural identity
hook: Empire is the default political form of human history. For most of the last 4,000 years, most of the world's population lived under some form of imperial rule — the dominance of one people, state, or dynasty over others who did not choose that dominance. The Roman Empire, the Mongol Empire, the Ottoman Empire, the British Empire: these are the structures through which civilizations connected, through which languages spread and died, through which trade routes were established and maintained, through which religions were propagated and suppressed. Empire built the modern world. Empire also built the modern world's inequalities. Understanding empire is understanding both things simultaneously.

beginner.theConcept: An empire is a political unit in which one group rules over other groups who did not choose that rule. The ruling group may control the others through military force, economic dominance, or the claim of superior civilization. What makes an empire different from other large states is the relationship between the ruler and the ruled. Empires are inherently unequal — built on the claim that some peoples have the right or capacity to govern others. This claim has been made in different ways across history, but the underlying structure is the same.
beginner.problemItSolves: Empires solve a specific political problem: how to organize and administer very large territories with diverse populations. Small states — city-states, tribal territories, early kingdoms — can be governed by personal relationships. Large territories cannot. Empires developed the bureaucratic, military, and administrative technologies for governing millions of people across thousands of miles: written laws, standardized currencies, roads and postal systems, professional armies, and tax collection systems. Every one of these administrative technologies was developed, refined, or massively scaled by empires.
beginner.howItWorks: Empires work through combination of force, administration, and ideology. Force establishes the initial dominance. Administration maintains it — collecting taxes, enforcing laws, moving troops. Ideology justifies it — the claim that imperial rule is legitimate, beneficial, divinely ordained, or simply inevitable. The Roman Empire used Roman law, Roman roads, and Roman citizenship. The British Empire used British law, British trade, and the ideology of civilizational improvement. The Mongol Empire used military terror, religious tolerance, and trade facilitation. Different empires relied on different combinations, but all required all three elements to function.
beginner.whatItExplains: Understanding empire explains several things that other frameworks cannot. Why do most of the world's languages look the way they do? Because empires spread languages — Latin produced French, Spanish, Portuguese, Italian, Romanian; Arabic spread with Islamic empire; Mandarin spread with Chinese imperial expansion. Why are national boundaries where they are? Because imperial powers drew them — often without regard for the populations affected. Why are some countries rich and others poor? Empire is part of the answer — the extraction of resources from colonial territories contributed to the wealth of imperial centers and the relative underdevelopment of colonial peripheries.
beginner.whereItBreaksDown: Empire as an analytical concept breaks down in two directions. Some arrangements that look like empires from outside do not look like empires from inside. The Chinese imperial tradition understood itself not as domination but as the natural extension of civilizational order to barbarian peripheries. Some arrangements that do not call themselves empires have many of empire's structural features. The contemporary United States maintains over 750 military bases abroad, controls global financial infrastructure, and has intervened militarily in dozens of countries without annexing them. Is that empire? The concept stretches at its edges.
beginner.usedAndMisusedC: Empire has been used as both a description and a condemnation, and the two uses are often confused. As a description, empire refers to the specific political form of multi-ethnic dominance. As a condemnation, empire implies illegitimate rule that should be resisted. The confusion between descriptive and evaluative uses makes serious analysis difficult. Defenders of imperial powers deny the descriptive label to avoid its implications. Critics apply the label to delegitimize arrangements that may not share all of empire's structural features. Careful use of the concept requires keeping the descriptive and evaluative questions separate, even while recognizing that they are related.

general.theConcept: The analytical definition of empire that most historians work with combines three elements: territorial extent, multi-ethnic composition, and hierarchical structure. An empire is a large territorial unit composed of diverse peoples governed by a structure in which the ruling center maintains distinct advantages over the periphery. This distinguishes empire from federations (where units have formal equality) and from nation-states (where the ideological claim is of shared identity between rulers and ruled). The definition remains broad enough to cover the full historical range — from the Akkadian Empire around 2334 BCE to the European colonial empires of the 19th and 20th centuries.
general.problemItSolves: Jane Burbank and Frederick Cooper's Empires in World History argues that empire has been the dominant political form of human history not because it was uniquely coercive but because it was uniquely flexible. Unlike nation-states, which require cultural homogenization, empires can govern diverse populations by recognizing difference — allowing different legal systems, religious practices, and administrative arrangements for different subject populations. The Ottoman millet system, which granted non-Muslim communities significant autonomy in matters of personal law, religion, and education, is the paradigm case. Empire's flexibility about cultural difference was part of its administrative utility.
general.howItWorks: Michael Doyle's taxonomy of imperial control distinguishes between formal empire (direct administrative control, colonial rule) and informal empire (economic and political influence without formal administration). The British Empire operated both modes simultaneously: formal empire in India, informal empire in China (where unequal treaties, concessions, and the threat of force maintained British commercial dominance without annexation). This distinction is analytically important because informal empire is harder to see, easier to deny, and more durable than formal empire — it can survive the formal end of colonial rule.
general.whatItExplains: The Columbian Exchange demonstrates what empires connect — and what those connections cost. European imperial expansion connected previously isolated disease pools, agricultural systems, and human populations. The consequences reshaped global demography, ecology, and economics. Understanding the Columbian Exchange requires understanding the imperial framework that enabled it. The specific form the Exchange took — who traveled where, with what resources, and what they brought — was determined by imperial political economy rather than by chance.
general.whereItBreaksDown: The concept of empire struggles with the question of continuity and change. When does a large state become an empire? When does an empire end? The Roman Empire's transformation into the Byzantine Empire into medieval European states is not a single clear break but a gradual change that the participants at each stage experienced as continuation rather than rupture. The British Empire's transformation into the Commonwealth into the current network of British influence is similarly continuous. Periodization debates about when empires begin and end are often debates about what the relevant continuities and discontinuities are.
general.usedAndMisusedC: The concept of "American empire" illustrates how empire is used both analytically and polemically. Scholars like Chalmers Johnson and Andrew Bacevich argue that the United States operates as an informal empire — through military bases, financial institutions, and alliance networks. Understanding American foreign policy as imperial, they argue, explains patterns that democracy-promotion rhetoric obscures. Critics argue that applying the empire label to democratic states misapplies the concept and serves anti-American polemics. The debate is simultaneously analytical (does the US meet the definition?) and political (does the label delegitimize appropriate forms of international engagement?).

advanced.theConcept: Hannah Arendt's account of the relationship between imperialism and totalitarianism in The Origins of Totalitarianism offers the most disturbing analytical connection between empire and the 20th century's worst political catastrophes. Arendt argues that European colonial imperialism developed the techniques of racial categorization, administrative dehumanization, and state violence that were later imported back to Europe as fascism and Nazism. The bureaucratic machinery of colonial administration — which categorized populations by race, administered them as resources rather than citizens, and applied violence systematically rather than individually — became, in Arendt's account, the template for totalitarian state practice. Empire did not just extract resources from colonial territories; it developed forms of political violence that eventually consumed the imperial powers themselves.
advanced.problemItSolves: Hardt and Negri's Empire (2000) argues that the classical form of empire — a single sovereign state exercising territorial dominance — has been superseded by a new, decentered form: Empire with a capital E, which is the distributed sovereignty of global capital, multilateral institutions, and superpower military dominance. On this account, the relevant political analysis is not about which state is the imperial center but about how global networks of power function without any single territorial center. The argument was influential and contested: it captured something real about post-Cold War globalization, while also being criticized for understating the continued centrality of specific states (particularly the United States) in the operation of global power.
advanced.howItWorks: Ranajit Guha's "dominance without hegemony" concept — developed in the context of British rule in India — identifies a specific imperial mode in which ruling classes maintain political dominance without achieving cultural hegemony: the ruled do not internalize the rulers' values and categories as their own. This is different from Gramsci's hegemony, in which ruling class values become naturalized as common sense. Guha argues that British colonial rule in India was characterized by the gap between the colonial state's coercive dominance and its failure to achieve the cultural penetration that would have made that dominance self-sustaining. This gap — between political control and cultural authority — is one of the reasons decolonization was possible: because colonial subjects never fully accepted the legitimacy of colonial rule, resistance was available even when it was suppressed.
advanced.whatItExplains: The political economy of imperial extraction — how empires take resources from peripheries and concentrate them in centers — is the most contested area in imperial studies because it directly implicates the question of contemporary global inequality. The dependency theory tradition (Frank, Wallerstein) argues that the wealth of currently rich countries was built substantially on colonial extraction from currently poor countries and that these structural relationships persist in the postcolonial global economy. Mainstream economics has generally resisted this claim, arguing that colonial extraction was often a net drain on imperial economies (imperial administration and military costs exceeded the economic benefits) and that current inequality reflects domestic institutional differences rather than colonial history. The empirical evidence is complex and contested; the political implications of each position are clear.
advanced.whereItBreaksDown: The concept of empire cannot easily accommodate the Chinese case. China's imperial tradition understood itself not as domination of others but as the extension of civilizational order — the "Middle Kingdom" conception in which China was the natural center of civilization and non-Chinese peoples were understood as barbaric not in a racial sense but in a cultural sense (they had not yet been civilized). Chinese imperial expansion was simultaneously political conquest and cultural incorporation: the goal was not to maintain permanent dominance over permanently distinct subject peoples but to civilize them into Chinese culture. This is a different logic from Roman or British empire, and it complicates the standard definition — while also raising questions about whether Chinese self-understanding accurately described what Chinese imperial expansion looked like from the perspective of those being incorporated.
advanced.usedAndMisusedC:
The question of reparations for colonial empire forces the analytical and evaluative questions about empire into direct contact. If empire involved systematic extraction from colonial territories that enriched imperial centers and impoverished colonial peripheries, and if those wealth relationships persist in the post-colonial world, then contemporary states that benefited from colonial extraction have some obligation to compensate states that were harmed by it. The analytical question (what were the actual economic effects of colonial rule?) and the evaluative question (what obligations do current generations have for historical injustices?) are both contested, but they cannot be separated in the reparations debate. The concept of empire is not just a historical category — it is a live political and legal question.

The confrontation this generates: two serious frameworks for understanding empire's legacy are incompatible in their policy implications. The continuity framework holds that colonial extraction created structural wealth inequalities that persist into the present — the current gap between rich and poor countries reflects the accumulated effects of centuries of imperial extraction, and reparations or redistribution are required to address them. The institutional framework holds that post-colonial development trajectories have been determined primarily by domestic institutional quality rather than by colonial history — countries that developed effective institutions after independence have prospered; countries that did not have not, and the explanation is primarily domestic. Both frameworks have empirical support; each explains something the other cannot. The debate cannot be resolved by economic evidence alone because it involves prior commitments about the time horizon over which historical injustice creates contemporary obligation.

foundation.theStory: Empire is not primarily an evil imposed on otherwise free peoples by uniquely wicked rulers. It is the dominant political form of most of human history — the structure through which most of the world's population lived, through which most of the world's trade moved, and through which most of the world's civilizational exchanges happened. Understanding empire requires holding its administrative achievements and its coercive foundations simultaneously — recognizing both what empire made possible and what it required.
foundation.theDebate: The central debate is about empire's legacy: did colonial empire create the contemporary global inequality between rich and poor countries, or did it accelerate development in some places while retarding it in others, with domestic institutional quality determining outcomes in both cases? This debate cannot be resolved without engaging both the economic evidence and the methodological question of how to construct the relevant counterfactual — what the colonized countries' economic trajectories would have been without empire.
foundation.whyItMatters: Empire matters because most of the world's current political geography, economic relationships, linguistic patterns, and cultural inheritances were shaped by imperial history. Understanding empire is understanding why the world looks the way it does — why national boundaries are where they are, why languages are distributed as they are, why wealth is concentrated where it is. Empire is not ancient history; it is the immediate predecessor of the present.
questions: Hannah Arendt argued that European colonial imperialism developed the techniques — racial categorization, administrative dehumanization, systematic state violence — that were later imported back to Europe as fascism and Nazism. If she is right, does that mean the Holocaust was not a European aberration but a European product — the methods of colonial empire applied within Europe? What are the implications of that claim?

Jane Burbank and Frederick Cooper argue that empire has been the dominant political form of human history precisely because it is more flexible than the nation-state — it can govern diverse populations without requiring cultural homogenization. Does that mean the nation-state ideal (one people, one culture, one state) is historically unusual and perhaps genuinely inferior to empire as a framework for governing diverse populations?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: Empire scholarship spans history (which empires, when, with what institutions), political science (how imperial systems function), economics (what imperial extraction produced), and postcolonial studies (how imperial ideologies shaped both colonial and colonized cultures). These disciplines use the concept of empire differently and for different purposes. Historians tend toward specificity; political scientists toward comparative generalization; postcolonial scholars toward ideological critique. All three perspectives are necessary; none is sufficient.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence for empire's effects is rich but methodologically challenging. The counterfactual — what would have happened without imperial rule — is inherently unobservable. Economic historians use a range of techniques (synthetic control methods, natural experiments) to estimate empire's economic effects, but all such estimates depend on assumptions about the relevant comparison and the relevant time horizon. The ideological effects of empire — on both colonizers and colonized — are even harder to measure but no less important.
interpretation.takingAPosition: The most defensible position is that empire is neither simply the engine of human civilization nor simply the greatest crime in human history — it is a political form with both genuine administrative achievements and genuine coercive foundations, whose effects varied enormously across different empires, different subject peoples, and different time periods. Understanding specific empires in their specific contexts is more illuminating than either blanket condemnation or blanket defense. The comparison between different empires — how they governed, what they extracted, what they left behind — is where the most useful analytical work happens.
questions: Ranajit Guha argues that British rule in India maintained political dominance without achieving cultural hegemony — the ruled never fully accepted the legitimacy of colonial rule. Does this suggest that colonial rule was more fragile than it appeared? And does it imply anything about the relationship between legitimate authority and durable political power?

The "American empire" debate asks whether the United States is an empire despite not governing formal colonies. What features of empire does the US possess, and which does it lack? Does the answer change anything practically — does it matter whether we call it empire if the effects are similar?

research.topic: The administrative technologies of ancient empires
research.content: The administrative innovations of ancient empires — the Achaemenid Persian road and postal system, the Roman legal system, the Chinese imperial examination and bureaucracy, the Mongol communications and taxation systems — are well-documented from archaeological and textual evidence. The comparative analysis of these innovations, and their transmission across imperial transitions, is a well-developed field in ancient and medieval history.

research.topic: The economic effects of colonial empire — mortality and displacement
research.content: The demographic consequences of European colonial empire — the mortality from introduced disease in the Americas, the Atlantic slave trade, the Indian famines under British rule, the Belgian Congo atrocities — are well-documented in historical and demographic scholarship. The specific death tolls are debated (estimates vary significantly) but the catastrophic scale of colonial violence is not. Mike Davis's Late Victorian Holocausts (2001) provides the most comprehensive account of famine mortality under colonial rule.

research.topic: The long-run economic effects of colonial rule on current inequality
research.content: The econometric literature on colonial legacy and current economic development is extensive but contested. Daron Acemoglu, Simon Johnson, and James Robinson's "reversal of fortune" papers argue that colonized societies with initially high population density (and therefore more intensive colonial extraction) are currently poorer than those with initially sparse populations (where European settler colonialism established better institutions). Nathan Nunn's work on the slave trade's effects on African economic development finds persistent negative effects. The specific mechanisms and their relative importance are still debated.

research.topic: The subjective experience of imperial rule — what it felt like to be governed by empire
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: Firsthand accounts from ordinary subjects of ancient and early modern empires describing their experience of imperial administration, tax collection, military conscription, and cultural impositions — not the accounts of elites who negotiated with imperial power but the experience of the majority who lived under it without choosing it. Why that evidence is unavailable: Most surviving records of imperial rule were produced by imperial administrators or by literate elites whose interests were partly aligned with the imperial system. The majority of imperial subjects were non-literate; their experience of empire left almost no direct written record. What can be inferred: From the pattern of rebellions, resistance, flight from imperial territories, and accommodation to imperial institutions, we can infer that imperial rule was experienced as a mixture of coercion, benefit, and accommodation that varied significantly by place, time, and position in the social hierarchy. What must remain genuinely unknowable: The phenomenology of being governed by an empire whose language, law, and culture were not your own — what it felt like to encounter Roman magistrates if you were a Gallic farmer, British district officers if you were a Bengali peasant, or Ottoman tax collectors if you were an Anatolian peasant.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Empire That Built Its Own Mythology
comparativeNarrative.content: The Roman Empire is the paradigm case for every subsequent European imperial tradition, not because it was the largest or most durable empire in history (it was neither) but because it became the model that European empires consciously imitated and against which they measured themselves. Napoleon crowned himself in a ceremony deliberately recalling the Roman Empire; British imperial administrators were educated in Latin and Roman history; American founders modeled constitutional institutions on their (partly mythologized) understanding of the Roman Republic. The Roman Empire's actual administrative achievements — Roman law, Roman roads, the spread of Latin as a legal and literary language — were real. But its cultural power over subsequent European empires was disproportionate to its actual historical record.

comparativeNarrative.name: Empire by Terror and Tolerance
comparativeNarrative.content: The Mongol Empire (1206–1368 CE) was the largest contiguous land empire in history — stretching from the Pacific to Eastern Europe at its height. Its methods of expansion combined extreme military violence (the destruction of Baghdad in 1258, the killing of millions in Central Asia and Persia) with a specific administrative philosophy: religious tolerance, promotion of trade, and skilled administrator recruitment regardless of origin. The Pax Mongolica — the period of relative peace and trade connectivity across the Mongol Empire — enabled the transmission of technologies, diseases, and people across Eurasia at a scale previously impossible. The same empire that destroyed cities and killed millions also enabled the Black Death's transmission westward and created the conditions for the Silk Road's most productive period.

comparativeNarrative.name: Empire as Multi-Ethnic Administration
comparativeNarrative.content: The Ottoman Empire (1299–1922 CE) represents one of the most sophisticated multi-ethnic imperial administrations in history. The millet system — which granted non-Muslim communities (Greeks, Armenians, Jews) significant autonomy in matters of personal law, religion, education, and community governance — allowed the empire to govern a diverse population without requiring cultural homogenization. The Ottoman model of recognized difference within imperial hierarchy is a genuine alternative to both the Roman assimilationist model and the European colonial model of racial hierarchy. Its late-Ottoman transformation into Turkish nationalism — and the genocides of Armenians, Greeks, and Assyrians that accompanied it — illustrates the violence that can result when an imperial system attempts to remake itself as a nation-state.

comparativeNarrative.name: The First Modern Global Empire
comparativeNarrative.content: The British Empire at its height (1815–1914) controlled approximately 25% of the world's land surface and 23% of its population — the largest empire in history by these measures. It operated through a combination of formal colonies (India, Australia, Canada), informal empire (China, Latin America), and a global financial and commercial system centered on the City of London. The British Empire simultaneously created global trade networks, spread the English language and British legal institutions across most of the world, abolished the Atlantic slave trade while maintaining racial hierarchy within its territories, and extracted enormous wealth from colonial territories. Its legacy — in the form of English as the global language, common law in former colonies, and the economic relationships that persist between Britain and its former empire — is more present in contemporary global life than any other empire.

rabbitHole.label: The Roman Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Roman Empire is the paradigm case for the analytical concept of empire — the model against which all subsequent European imperial projects measured themselves and from which most political analysis of empire derives

rabbitHole.label: The Mongol Empire
rabbitHole.reason: The Mongol Empire represents the most extreme version of empire's basic trade-offs: maximum military violence combined with maximum administrative tolerance — essential for understanding empire's full range

rabbitHole.label: Colonialism
rabbitHole.reason: Colonialism is the specific modern form of empire — empire organized around racial hierarchy, systematic extraction, and the ideology of civilizational improvement — and understanding the relationship between empire and colonialism is central to understanding contemporary global inequality

rabbitHole.label: Nationalism
rabbitHole.reason: Nationalism is the primary ideology through which empires have been challenged and dissolved — the claim that each people has the right to its own state directly contradicts the imperial claim that some peoples have the right to govern others

rabbitHole.label: Law
rabbitHole.reason: Law is the primary administrative technology of empire — the mechanism through which imperial rule is normalized, made predictable, and differentiated from mere coercion

reference.author: Jane Burbank and Frederick Cooper
reference.title: Empires in World History: Power and the Politics of Difference
reference.annotation: The most comprehensive and analytically sophisticated comparative history of empires — argues that empire's flexibility about difference (rather than the nation-state's demand for homogeneity) explains its historical durability.

reference.author: Hannah Arendt
reference.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
reference.annotation: The political theory account of how imperialism's administrative innovations in colonial territories were imported back to Europe as totalitarianism — one of the most disturbing and important arguments in 20th-century political thought.

reference.author: Ranajit Guha
reference.title: Dominance without Hegemony: History and Power in Colonial India
reference.annotation: The postcolonial theory account of British imperial rule in India — argues that colonial dominance never achieved cultural hegemony, leaving a permanent gap between political control and legitimate authority that made decolonization possible.

reference.author: Mike Davis
reference.title: Late Victorian Holocausts: El Niño Famines and the Making of the Third World
reference.annotation: The account of famine mortality under British colonial rule — documents the systematic relationship between imperial political economy, climate events, and mass death in India, China, and elsewhere in the late 19th century.

commerce.title: Empires in World History
commerce.author: Jane Burbank and Frederick Cooper
commerce.note: The essential comparative history — covers all major empires from Rome to the Cold War and argues for empire's historical logic rather than treating it as simply anomalous evil

commerce.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism
commerce.author: Hannah Arendt
commerce.note: The disturbing argument — how imperialism developed the administrative technologies of dehumanization that were later applied within Europe

commerce.title: Late Victorian Holocausts
commerce.author: Mike Davis
commerce.note: The economic account — how imperial political economy combined with climate events to produce mass death at a scale that requires reckoning with

popularCulture.title: The Crown (2016–2023)
popularCulture.description: Netflix's The Crown dramatizes the British monarchy from World War II through the early 21st century — and is implicitly a drama about the British Empire's decline. The series depicts the end of formal colonial rule, the creation of the Commonwealth, and the persistence of British imperial self-image as the actual imperial power was transferred or dissolved. The Crown makes visible the emotional and political difficulty of post-imperial identity for a country that still occupied a permanent UN Security Council seat but had ceded economic and military primacy to the United States.

popularCulture.title: Gandhi (1982)
popularCulture.description: Richard Attenborough's Gandhi dramatizes Mahatma Gandhi's leadership of Indian independence and gives the most widely seen depiction of British imperial rule in India from the colonial subject's perspective. The film frames the independence struggle through Gandhi's nonviolent resistance doctrine and his eventual assassination, presenting empire as simultaneously powerful and morally vulnerable — its coercive foundations exposed by the moral authority of those resisting it. Gandhi remains the primary popular culture depiction of how empires end from the inside.

popularCulture.title: The White Man's Burden — Kipling's poem and its legacy
popularCulture.description: Rudyard Kipling's 1899 poem "The White Man's Burden" — written to encourage the United States to take up colonial rule of the Philippines — is the most concise expression of the ideological justification for European colonial empire: the claim that white Europeans had a duty to civilize non-European peoples who were not yet capable of self-governance. The poem's subsequent history — as an object of mockery, condemnation, and scholarly analysis — illustrates how imperial ideology works: it frames coercive dominance as benevolent service, extraction as investment, and resistance as ingratitude.

popularCulture.title: The debate about American empire
popularCulture.description: Since the 1990s, scholars and journalists have debated whether the United States operates as an informal empire — maintaining over 750 military bases in foreign countries, controlling global financial infrastructure through the dollar and multilateral institutions, and intervening militarily in dozens of countries without formally annexing them. The debate is simultaneously analytical (does the US meet any definition of empire?) and political (does calling it empire delegitimize US foreign policy or clarify its actual structure?). Chalmers Johnson's trilogy (Blowback, The Sorrows of Empire, Nemesis) and Niall Ferguson's Empire are the most influential popular treatments from opposite political directions.

popularCulture.title: The British Museum — imperial collection and the repatriation debate
popularCulture.description: The British Museum holds approximately 8 million objects from across the world, many collected during the period of British imperial expansion. The Elgin Marbles (sculptures from the Parthenon), the Rosetta Stone, the Benin Bronzes (looted from the Kingdom of Benin in 1897), and thousands of other objects are held in London while their countries of origin request their return. The British Museum's collection is the most visible embodiment of the material legacy of empire — and the repatriation debate is the most concrete contemporary political dispute about what obligations current institutions have for historical imperial acquisitions.
